g.data-narration: A file is a named collection of related information stored on secondary storage. The OS abstracts physical properties of storage into a logical unit called a file.
g.text: Named collection of related data stored on secondary storage

g.data-narration: File attributes: name is the human-readable identifier. Type indicates file format. Location is pointer to device and position. Size is current file size. Protection controls access rights.
File operations: create, write, read, reposition (seek), delete, and truncate. These six basic operations combine to perform all file manipulations needed by users and programs.
g.text: Create: allocate space, add to directory

g.data-narration: Open file table: OS maintains system-wide and per-process tables. Open returns file descriptor or handle. Table stores file pointer, access rights, location, and open count.
g.text: File pointer, access rights, disk location

g.data-narration: File types: indicated by extension. Executable, object, source code, batch, text, word processor, library, print, archive, multimedia. OS may or may not enforce types.
g.text: Some OS enforce, others use magic numbers

g.data-narration: File structure: simple sequence of bytes, sequence of records, or complex tree structure. Unix treats files as byte streams. OS determines internal structure or leaves it to application.
File locking: shared lock for concurrent reads, exclusive lock for writes. Advisory locks are optional. Mandatory locks enforced by OS. Prevents data corruption during concurrent access.
Files are the fundamental abstraction for persistent storage. Attributes, operations, types, and locking mechanisms provide a complete interface for data management.
g.text: Attributes + Operations + Types + Locking = Complete File Abstraction
